title: Today's Tech Brief: Meta made its own AI-generated clickbait news feed
summary: Meta's AI-generated clickbait news feed, free open source models powering computer worms, and the implications for tech
imageAlt: Image from theverge.com for Meta made its own AI-generated clickbait news feed
imageCaption: Meta made its own AI-generated clickbait news feed (theverge.com)

body.text: I'm checking out a couple of tech stories that caught my eye. First, Meta created its own AI-generated clickbait news feed, which is exactly what it sounds like - a feed of articles designed to grab your attention. According to The Verge, Meta quickly pulled the feature after they asked some questions about it. I suppose you could call it a 'clickbait fail'.

In another story, researchers discovered that free open source models can be used to build computer worms that cause chaos. No expensive tools or fancy '0-days' needed. This is interesting because it shows how easily attackers can now use known vulnerabilities at scale. It's a reminder that 'innovation' isn't always a positive word.

A third story that's been on my mind is the broader implications of these developments. With AI-generated content and easily accessible tools, the barriers to entry for creating complex systems - both legitimate and malicious - are lower than ever. I'm not sure what the future holds, but it's clear that we're going to see more of this kind of thing.

My read on all this is that it's a wild time to be in tech. The lines between innovation and concern are blurring, and it's hard to predict what will happen next. One thing's for sure - I'll be keeping an eye on these developments.
